text: The most important predictor variables for half and full marathon performance are laboratory-derived measures (VO2max, vVO2max), training variables (training pace, training load), and anthropometric variables (fat mass, skinfolds).

Among variables associated with long-distance running performance, 43.4% are derived from aerobic metabolism evaluation, 26.5% from training load, and 20.6% from anthropometric variables, body composition, and somatotype.

Half-marathon performance prediction relies mainly on anthropometric variables, but these yield only moderate coefficients of determination, indicating limited predictive power from body composition alone.
Marathon performance is best predicted by training-related variables (training pace, training load) combined with physiological evaluation and anthropometric parameters.
Field-test-based prediction models for long-distance race performance remain underdeveloped, with physiological effort assessments almost exclusively used for shorter events (5000m and 10,000m).
Velocity at VO2max (vVO2max) is among the strongest laboratory-derived predictors of half-marathon and marathon performance, alongside VO2max itself.
